title: Atlas of Adaptive Reading
subtitle: A handbook for moving through idea webs instead of static pages
description: This sample book shows how chapters, jump links, cheat sheets, and appendices can support an AI-generated book that feels more like a living reference.

cheatSheet.reminder: Start from the question the reader has, not from the canonical first page.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when the reader already knows part of the topic and wants a direct route.

cheatSheet.reminder: Short sections should point outward to the next useful concept.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when a concept only makes sense in relation to another chapter.

cheatSheet.reminder: A cheat sheet compresses a whole chapter into a quick reminder layer.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when returning readers need a fast refresher.

cheatSheet.reminder: Keep glossary material and supporting evidence in the appendix so the main path stays light.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when detail is important but not always needed on the first pass.

appendix.title: Reader mode glossary
items: Bridge: a link that explains why another section matters next.
Recall layer: a compressed review surface such as a cheat sheet.
Concept map: a visual arrangement of chapters and their relationships.

appendix.title: Design cues
items: Prefer stable IDs so saved progress survives future edits.
Keep chapter summaries short enough to scan from a sidebar.
Make map positions feel spatially meaningful rather than decorative.

chapters.title: Entry points
chapters.summary: Readers should be able to begin from intent, not only from page one.

sections.heading: Ask-first reading
sections.lede: A connected book becomes easier to use when the first interaction is a question, not a table of pages.
body: Traditional reading apps assume every reader wants the same route. A reference-style book accepts that one person arrives to understand a missing concept while another returns to check a detail they already half remember.
That means the opening state should surface chapter summaries, search-like entry points, and routes that are framed around goals instead of chronology.

callout.label: Pattern
callout.text: Treat the first screen like a launch pad: show where each chapter leads, not just where it sits in order.

links.label: Open the chapter web
links.reason: Turn an ordered table of contents into a route map.

sections.heading: Skim for bridges
sections.lede: Sections should help the reader decide what to jump to next before they commit to a long scroll.
body: A useful skim layer includes concise summaries, visible chapter relationships, and small pieces of evidence that reveal which chapter unlocks the next question.
The payoff is speed: readers can move laterally across the book instead of repeatedly starting over from the beginning.

callout.label: Why it matters
callout.text: Bridge text explains why a jump is worth taking, which makes the graph feel editorial rather than random.

links.label: See recall layers
links.reason: Bridge sections work better when readers can also drop into fast reminders.

chapters.title: Chapter web
chapters.summary: The table of contents can behave like a map when chapter relationships are explicit.

sections.heading: Turn the table of contents into a map
sections.lede: A chapter list becomes more powerful when it shows adjacency and progress, not only order.
body: Most tables of contents answer the question "what comes next?" A map adds two more answers: "what is nearby?" and "what should I open if I am stuck here?"
Showing chapter completion and concept links together encourages readers to move between ideas with confidence instead of losing context.

callout.label: Design cue
callout.text: Maps do not replace the table of contents; they enrich it with relationship data.

links.label: Return to entry points
links.reason: Map design starts with the reader's opening question.

sections.heading: Link chapters with reasons
sections.lede: A jump becomes trustworthy when the UI explains why the next chapter is relevant.
body: Unexplained links look decorative. Reasoned links feel editorial: they tell the reader whether the next chapter offers background, contrast, evidence, or a faster summary.
This is how a knowledge graph stops being a novelty and becomes a real navigation tool.

callout.label: Constraint
callout.text: Avoid linking every chapter to every other chapter. A dense graph without editorial intent is harder to use than a simple list.

links.label: Use recall layers to compress routes
links.reason: Summary surfaces prevent the graph from forcing long detours.

links.label: Prepare a handoff prompt
links.reason: Bookmarks are more useful when the next action is clearly named.

chapters.title: Recall layers
chapters.summary: Cheat sheets and appendices help readers re-enter the book without rereading every chapter.
keyIdeas: Separate fast review from deep detail

sections.heading: Cheat-sheet mode
sections.lede: A cheat sheet is the book's quick-memory layer: small, portable, and closer to retrieval than discovery.
body: Readers often revisit a book because they remember the shape of an idea but not the precise phrase or next move. Cheat sheets answer that need directly.
In an AI-generated book, the cheat sheet also provides a compact summary the model can preserve when regenerating adjacent sections.

callout.label: Shortcut
callout.text: If a chapter cannot produce a sharp cheat-sheet reminder, the chapter may still be too fuzzy.

links.label: Map the route first
links.reason: The best recall shortcuts still need clear onward routes.

sections.heading: Appendix traces
sections.lede: Keep definitions, source trails, and operating notes nearby without making every section heavy.
body: An appendix works well when it behaves like a support shelf: always available, rarely in the way. It carries durable details while the main text stays focused on movement between ideas.
This split is especially useful for generated books because the appendix can stabilize repeated concepts across versions.

links.label: Package the next step as a prompt
links.reason: Appendix notes become actionable when they are paired with a clear resume instruction.

chapters.title: Handoff prompts
chapters.summary: Readers should be able to leave and return with enough context to continue instantly.

sections.heading: Bookmarks as next-step prompts
sections.lede: A bookmark becomes more useful when it stores what the reader intended to do next.
body: Instead of saving only position, save the chapter and section that best explain the reader's current task. This creates a bookmark with narrative momentum.
When progress is local-first, this handoff stays lightweight and private while still helping the reader restart quickly.

callout.label: Implementation hint
callout.text: Keep saved progress tied to stable chapter and section IDs so future schema changes stay compatible.

links.label: Review the cheat sheet
links.reason: A bookmark is strongest when a quick refresher is one tap away.

sections.heading: Exit with context
sections.lede: The best reader state captures enough context that returning feels like continuing, not restarting.
body: Progress alone tells the reader where they were. Context tells them why they were there. Together they create a more generous return experience.
This is one of the clearest advantages of a local PWA: it can quietly remember orientation cues without requiring a backend or account.

links.label: Revisit the first entry point
links.reason: Good exits mirror good entrances by exposing the next useful route.
